pricing: Free tier (up to 250 profiles, 500 emails/mo). Email plans scale by active profiles: ~$20/mo at 500 contacts, ~$100/mo at 5,000, ~$150/mo at 10,000, ~$720/mo at 50,000. SMS is a separate add-on subscription. Note: since Feb 2025 billing is based on all active profiles, not just contacts you email.
bestFor: Independents and small groups that want serious email + SMS automation (win-backs, segmentation) without an enterprise contract.
pros: Genuinely free to start and grow into
Best-in-class segmentation and automated flows (win-back, birthday, lapsed)
Combined email + SMS with strong reporting
Wide POS/ecommerce integrations
cons: Active-profile billing means a big list costs you even when you don't email everyone
SMS is billed as a separate subscription plus per-message credits
Built for ecommerce first; restaurant features are not native
integrations: Connects to many POS and online-ordering/ecommerce platforms; Shopify and Square among the most common. SMS, email, and WhatsApp run under the platform.
verdict: The most capable email + SMS engine you can start free, but watch the active-profile billing as your list grows. Great for operators willing to learn it; overkill if you just want simple texts.

pricing: Quote-only. Typically ~$300/mo base plus ~$0.01/SMS and carrier fees, with quarterly minimums of roughly $2,000-$3,000 (about $667-$1,000/mo before you send anything). A dedicated short code adds ~$500/mo. Contracts usually run 6-12 months; reported annual deals range widely (median around $40k).
bestFor: High-volume, multi-location brands where SMS already drives a meaningful share of revenue.
pros: Enterprise-grade SMS deliverability and list growth tools
Strong AI-assisted campaign and audience features
Scales to very large subscriber bases
Adds email alongside SMS
cons: High minimums and multi-month contracts
Opaque, sales-led pricing
Too costly for a single independent location
integrations: Integrates with major ecommerce and marketing stacks; restaurant fit depends on your POS/ordering connections.
verdict: Powerful SMS at scale, but the minimums and contracts make it a multi-unit play. A single family restaurant will get more value from Klaviyo or SlickText.

pricing: Plans start around $29/mo for ~500 credits, ~$49/mo for double that, $129-$169/mo tiers with onboarding for growing senders, up to ~$939/mo for 50,000 credits. Annual billing adds two free months and a year of rollover. Short codes and dedicated numbers cost extra.
bestFor: Single sites and small groups that want straightforward, affordable text marketing without contracts.
pros: Transparent, low-cost entry pricing
Simple keywords and automation that are easy to learn
Rollover credits so you don't lose what you pay for
No enterprise contract required
cons: SMS-focused; email is light or absent
Per-credit model penalizes very high send volume
Fewer advanced segmentation/automation features than Klaviyo
integrations: Connects to common business tools and offers an API; restaurant POS integration is more limited than restaurant-native platforms.
verdict: An honest, affordable way to run text campaigns for an independent. If you also need email and deep automation, look at Klaviyo or a loyalty-bundled tool like Marsello.

pricing: Loyalty Launch around $60/mo and Loyalty Accelerate around $120/mo (prices quoted in NZD; SMS billed in USD), with an Enterprise tier. Marketing, SMS, and Social are paid add-ons that stack. Each extra location adds roughly $100-$200/mo depending on plan. 14-day free trial.
bestFor: Hospitality and retail operators who want loyalty plus email/SMS marketing in one POS-connected tool.
pros: Loyalty and marketing live together, so campaigns target real spend
Connects to common POS and ecommerce systems
Reasonable entry price for an all-in-one
No long contract; cancel anytime
cons: Marketing and SMS are add-ons that raise the real monthly cost
Per-location pricing climbs for multi-site
Strongest as a loyalty tool; pure-marketing depth trails specialists
integrations: Integrates with Lightspeed, Square, Shopify and other POS/ecommerce platforms; built for retail and hospitality.
verdict: A tidy way to combine loyalty and marketing if you're already on a supported POS. Just price out the add-ons before you commit, because they stack.

pricing: Published rates: $99/mo for a single location, or about $60/mo per location for 2-10 locations, dropping toward $25/mo per location at 100+ (higher tiers quoted). Includes ~3,000 email and ~300 SMS credits per location monthly. Add-ons: Listings Hub ~$40/mo per location, promotional SMS ~$10/mo per location.
bestFor: Independents and small groups that want transparent, affordable review generation with on-brand reply help.
pros: Publishes its prices upfront, which is rare in this category
Generates reviews and drafts replies you can approve
Email and SMS credits included in the base plan
Per-location cost drops sharply as you add sites
cons: Add-ons (listings, promo SMS) push the real cost higher
Less restaurant-specific than survey-first tools
Bigger brands see less rating-to-revenue lift than independents
integrations: Connects to Google and other review/listing platforms; offers white-label options for agencies.
verdict: One of the few review tools that shows its pricing, and it's fair for a small operator. For an independent, a one-star rating lift can mean real revenue, so the payback is easy to justify.

pricing: Quote-only (existing public estimates put it around $79-$259/mo per location; confirm on a live quote). Custom quotes typically returned within 24 hours; multi-location and ghost-kitchen pricing available.
bestFor: Restaurants that want to catch unhappy guests privately before they post and steer happy ones to public reviews.
pros: Two-question SMS survey drives very high response rates
Routes unhappy guests to private recovery, happy ones to public reviews
AI insights and (as of late 2025) built-in reputation/listings management
Integrates with 50+ POS, ordering, and loyalty systems
cons: Pricing is quote-only, so you need a sales conversation
Lower tiers historically gate review-response and deeper insights
Best value when you actively work the guest-recovery flow
integrations: Connects to 50+ POS, online-ordering, and loyalty platforms; recently added reviews, listings, and social into one ecosystem.
verdict: A strong guest-recovery and review-generation tool, especially the two-question SMS approach. The main friction is having to get a quote, but it pays back when you actually use the recovery loop.

pricing: Base ~$74.99/mo per location, Pro ~$119.99/mo, Pro+Advanced ~$149.99/mo, with custom pricing for 5+ locations. Optional done-for-you review-response bundles (AI + human) priced by review volume, up to ~500 responses/mo.
bestFor: Operators who want listings, menus, and reviews kept accurate and answered across every platform from one dashboard.
pros: Syncs menus and listings to Google, Yelp, Facebook, Apple Maps and 50+ platforms
POS menu changes update everywhere automatically
AI + human review responses available as a bundle
Transparent per-location pricing
cons: Listings/menu-first; lighter on structured guest surveys
Review-response volume bundles add cost
Per-location pricing adds up for many sites
integrations: Direct POS and online-ordering integrations plus a Yext partnership for listings; pushes to Google, Yelp, Facebook, Apple Maps and more.
verdict: The best pick if your real pain is wrong hours and stale menus across the web as much as reviews. It keeps your digital presence consistent, but pair it with a survey tool if guest feedback is the priority.

pricing: Quote-only. Reported plans start around $399/mo (Core), $599/mo (Pro), and $999+/mo (Enterprise). AI review replies add ~$99/mo; extra locations ~$50/mo each and extra users ~$25-$30/mo. Most single sites end up around $500-$800/mo after add-ons.
bestFor: Operators who want reviews and all customer messaging (SMS, web chat, Google, Facebook) consolidated with AI in one inbox.
pros: Unified inbox for reviews and two-way customer messaging
Automated SMS review requests that convert well
AI replies and an AI phone/answer agent on higher tiers
Pulls reviews from Google, Facebook and more into one place
cons: Add-ons (AI replies, 10DLC, extra users/locations) push the real cost well past the base
No public pricing; sales-led
Pricey for a single independent versus simpler review tools
integrations: Connects to Google Business, Facebook, and SMS/web-chat channels; integrates with many CRMs and business tools.
verdict: A capable reviews-plus-messaging hub, but the true cost lands higher than the headline once you add AI and extra seats. Best for businesses that genuinely want to centralize all customer texting, not just reviews.

pricing: Quote-only with prepaid annual tiers: Starter ~$299/mo per location, Growth ~$399/mo, Dominate ~$499/mo. Monthly billing runs higher. Per-location cost drops with more locations; onboarding may apply.
bestFor: Multi-location brands that need review generation, monitoring, and listings managed across many sites.
pros: Built for managing reputation across many locations
Strong review generation, monitoring, and AI replies
Adds listings, surveys, social listening, and competitor monitoring at higher tiers
Well-rated enterprise platform with deep reporting
cons: Per-location pricing plus onboarding is heavy for a single site
Initial setup complexity
Quote-based; no transparent self-serve pricing
integrations: Integrates with Google, Yelp, Facebook and 200+ review/listing sites, plus many CRM and business systems.
verdict: A robust, enterprise-leaning reputation platform that shines across multiple locations. For one independent restaurant it's likely more tool and cost than you need.

pricing: Per location per month, starting from $59/location, with lower per-location rates as you add sites. White-glove onboarding (setup specialist, custom surveys, integration, training) included; billing adjusts automatically as you add or remove locations.
bestFor: Multi-unit fast-casual and full-service operators who want to pinpoint exactly what to fix to lift satisfaction.
pros: Causation-based surveys tie feedback to specific operational drivers
Transparent per-location pricing from $59
Integrates with POS, ordering, and loyalty for higher response
Onboarding and mobile app included at no extra cost
cons: Feedback/analytics focus, not public-review generation
Most valuable at multi-unit scale where you can act on patterns
Newer reputation/review-management features still maturing
integrations: Connects to POS, online-ordering, and loyalty systems; integrates with platforms like Incentivio.
verdict: The right tool when you want to know precisely what's dragging scores down, not just collect star ratings. Pair it with a review-generation tool if growing public reviews is also a goal.

pricing: Square Loyalty runs ~$45/mo as a POS add-on (Square Marketing/email is a further ~$15/mo). Toast Loyalty is bundled into Toast's Marketing package at ~$185/mo on top of your Toast POS subscription, so all-in is often $250-$350+/mo.
bestFor: Restaurants already on Toast or Square that want simple, no-fuss loyalty tied to the POS.
pros: Native to your POS, so setup and tracking are easy
Square's add-on is genuinely cheap to start
Points/visits loyalty that drives repeat visits
One vendor, one bill
cons: Basic feature set; no real AI personalization
Data is locked to that POS, and switching loses it
Toast's loyalty is bundled into a pricier marketing package
integrations: Tightly integrated with Toast POS or Square POS respectively; limited use outside that ecosystem.
verdict: The path of least resistance if you're already on Toast or Square. It's basic, but for a small shop that just wants a points program, that's often enough.

pricing: Quote-only; widely cited starting around $79/mo, with the real cost rising as you add features like mobile ordering, flash sales, and SMS. No transparent public tiers.
bestFor: Independent restaurants and small chains that want a feature-rich, fully branded loyalty program.
pros: Branded loyalty with promotions, flash sales, and surveys
Includes mobile-ordering and review-management options
Flexible enough for single sites and small multi-location
Customer-friendly, hardware-supported tablet experience
cons: No public pricing; you must talk to sales
Real cost climbs as add-ons stack
Can be more than a tiny shop needs
integrations: Integrates with many POS systems and supports its own mobile ordering and marketing channels.
verdict: A capable, customizable loyalty platform for independents who want more than a basic points card. Get a full quote with the add-ons you actually need before comparing.

pricing: Starts around $249/mo plus setup fees; higher tiers and custom pricing for multi-unit. Combines online ordering, loyalty, marketing automation, and analytics in one platform.
bestFor: Multi-unit restaurants that want a branded app, ordering, and loyalty unified in one commission-free platform.
pros: White-label app and web ordering plus loyalty in one system
Points-, visit-, or spend-based rewards with tiers
Marketing automation and guest analytics included
Commission-free ordering keeps more of each sale
cons: Setup fees on top of the monthly cost
Depth and price overshoot most single-unit operators
Best ROI requires meaningful digital-order volume
integrations: Integrates with POS systems and delivery/ordering channels; built to centralize digital ordering and guest data.
verdict: A solid all-in-one for small chains wanting their own ordering app plus loyalty without enterprise pricing. A single independent should weigh the setup fee and monthly against simpler options.

pricing: Quote-only; existing estimates put it roughly $100-$300/mo per location, depending on features and number of locations. No public tiers; price set after a sales consultation.
bestFor: Restaurants wanting AI-personalized, gamified loyalty and a branded app without jumping to full enterprise scale.
pros: Personalized, data-driven offers (cashback, spin-to-win, targeted deals)
Branded app and deep POS integration
Gamified mechanics that drive engagement
Scales from single sites to multi-unit
cons: Quote-only pricing requires a sales process
Can be more platform than a very small shop needs
Setup and configuration take some effort
integrations: Deep POS integrations; positioned for food-and-beverage operators across single and multi-unit.
verdict: A strong middle option: more personalization and gamification than POS-native loyalty, without the enterprise price of Paytronix or Thanx. The catch is you'll need a quote to evaluate it.

pricing: Quote-only, custom-priced by location count, modules, and contract terms; no free plan. Existing estimates put it in the high-hundreds to low-thousands per month per location for full suites.
bestFor: High-frequency, multi-unit brands that want loyalty, CRM, ordering, and AI personalization in one deep platform.
pros: Among the deepest loyalty and CRM capabilities available
Unifies loyalty, online ordering, and AI personalization
Strong one-to-one offer targeting at scale
Proven with large restaurant and convenience brands
cons: Enterprise-oriented; opaque, custom pricing
Implementation and ongoing support add to total cost
Overkill for single independents
integrations: Broad POS and online-ordering integrations; built as the data and loyalty backbone for multi-unit brands.
verdict: A gold-standard enterprise loyalty and CRM suite for brands with scale and a marketing team to run it. Not aimed at a single family restaurant.

pricing: Quote-only, subscription/quotation-based; typically multi-year (often 3-year) commitments with extra fees for dedicated success management and support. No free plan.
bestFor: Established restaurant chains (roughly 40+ locations) wanting agentic AI marketing and frictionless data capture.
pros: Frictionless in-store data capture via credit-card linking
Agentic AI that runs personalized campaigns autonomously
Strong loyalty + CRM + digital experience in one
Restaurant-specific design and deep integrations
cons: Long (often 3-year) contracts
Enterprise pricing; support tiers cost extra
Not aimed at single independents
integrations: Integrates with Toast and other POS systems, web/app ordering, and via API into existing digital experiences.
verdict: Powerful, modern loyalty for chains that want AI to run campaigns for them. The multi-year contract and enterprise focus rule it out for small operators.

pricing: Quote-only, per-store per-month subscription with no integration or implementation fees (a free trial is available). Existing estimate is around $200/mo per location; confirm on a quote.
bestFor: Multi-unit brands that want one customer data platform unifying POS, ordering, payments, and loyalty data.
pros: Restaurant-specific CDP that ties marketing/menu decisions to guest behavior
No integration or implementation fees
Unifies fragmented data into one guest view
Free trial to evaluate
cons: CDP/analytics, not a loyalty or marketing engine on its own
Built for multi-unit brands, not single shops
Public pricing not disclosed
integrations: Connects to POS, online-ordering, payments, and loyalty systems to centralize guest data.
verdict: The data backbone for serious multi-unit marketing, valuable precisely because it has no setup fees. For a single location it's the wrong layer of the stack.

pricing: Tiered per location: Operator/Starter around $179-$199/mo (50 invoice scans/mo), Growth around $249/mo (unlimited scans, advanced automation), Enterprise custom. Annual billing discounts apply; setup fees and add-ons increase total. A Square-bundled version runs ~$99/mo per location.
bestFor: Full-service and fast-casual operators who want to tighten ordering, recipe costing, and food cost across vendors.
pros: Inventory, purchasing, and recipe costing in one POS-synced system
Invoice scanning and vendor management automate back-office work
Real-time food cost visibility
Available as a lower-cost Square add-on
cons: Install/setup fee plus per-location cost stings a tiny single shop
Invoice-scan limits on the lower tier
Best automation features gated to higher tiers
integrations: Syncs with major POS systems and suppliers; available as Square Restaurant Inventory by MarketMan.
verdict: A strong inventory and purchasing platform that pays back through tighter ordering and food cost. Just price the setup fee and the right tier, since scan limits matter.

pricing: Flat $350/mo per location (10% off with annual billing). Bill Pay is free and unlimited for US clients; no traditional setup fees (onboarding packages instead). Optional Freepour add-on ~$150/mo per location. Toast users pay Toast ~$50/mo per location for API access.
bestFor: Single sites and small groups that want invoice automation, live food cost, and bill pay without per-unit games.
pros: Flat, predictable per-location pricing
Automates invoice entry and shows daily food cost
Free, unlimited bill pay for US clients
No traditional setup fees
cons: Flat fee can outrun savings for a very small, low-volume QSR
Toast users incur an extra Toast API fee
Beverage/Freepour costing is a paid add-on
integrations: Integrates with most major POS systems (with a small extra fee on Toast) and accounting platforms like QuickBooks and Sage Intacct.
verdict: Excellent at killing back-office hours and shaving food cost, with refreshingly simple flat pricing. Best once your volume is high enough that the savings clear the $350/mo.

pricing: Very low entry: listing/access plans cited around $10/mo, with a higher tier around $200/mo, plus custom plans. Pricing is not fully transparent for the buyer side; confirm scope on a quote.
bestFor: Operators who mainly want to streamline supplier ordering and procurement at a low entry price.
pros: Low cost to get started
Centralizes ordering across multiple suppliers in one place
Order, delivery, and invoice matching automated
Adds inventory plus newer predictive ordering and menu-profitability tools
cons: Procurement-first; lighter on deep recipe/food-cost analysis
Pricing scope not fully transparent
Best value depends on your suppliers being on the platform
integrations: Connects buyers and food-and-beverage distributors on one platform; integrates ordering, inventory, and invoicing.
verdict: A cheap, practical way to consolidate supplier ordering, especially if your vendors already use it. If costing depth is the goal, pair it with MarketMan or MarginEdge.

pricing: Quote-only. Marketplace per-order fees were discontinued in 2023; pricing now centers on its software modules (Order Manager, Invoice Manager, AP Manager). Contact sales for current rates.
bestFor: Operators (especially in Canada) who want supplier ordering, invoicing, and AP automation in one place.
pros: Centralizes supplier catalogs, ordering, and reorders
Automates invoice and AP workflows for better cash flow
Full line-of-sight into purchases and order status
No per-order marketplace fees
cons: Strongest in Canada; US coverage thinner
Ordering/AP-focused, lighter on recipe costing
Opaque, quote-only pricing
integrations: Works with existing distributor systems; connects restaurants and distributors plus AP/accounting workflows.
verdict: A clean choice for centralizing supplier ordering and accounts payable, particularly for Canadian operators. Get a quote and confirm your suppliers are on it before committing.

pricing: Quote-only, custom by location count and modules. Existing estimates put it roughly $400-$800/mo per location for full deployments; used across 100,000+ locations worldwide.
bestFor: Large chains and franchises that need enterprise-grade inventory, ordering, and food-cost (AvT) control at scale.
pros: Gold-standard inventory and food-cost control for big operators
Actual-vs-theoretical (AvT) food cost management
Unifies inventory, labor/scheduling, learning, and food safety
Proven with major national brands
cons: Built for large chains, not small shops
Custom pricing and longer implementation
Some users find the cost hard to justify below scale
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems and supplier networks; part of a broader restaurant operations suite (which now includes QSR Automations).
verdict: The enterprise standard for controlling food and labor at scale across many locations. Overkill and overpriced for an independent or a handful of sites.

pricing: Standard plan around $149/mo per location, with lower entry pricing reportedly from ~$79/mo. Free trial with no credit card required; per-location subscription model.
bestFor: QSR, fast-casual, and full-service operators who want AI sales and labor forecasts to staff and prep to real demand.
pros: AI forecasts of sales, labor, and item-level demand
Factors in weather, events, and history
Affordable per-location pricing with a free trial
Real-time dashboards and variance analytics
cons: Forecasting-focused; not a full inventory or scheduling suite
Needs roughly six months of data for best accuracy
Value depends on acting on the forecasts
integrations: Connects to POS systems and labor-scheduling tools to pull history and push forecasts.
verdict: An affordable, restaurant-specific forecasting tool that helps you stop staffing and prepping by gut. Worth it once you have the history to feed it and a plan to act on the numbers.

pricing: Starts around $600/year; priced per module and per location, so cost climbs with scale. Most operators recoup it by catching one area of waste; typically live within two weeks.
bestFor: Multi-site fast-casual and full-service operators who want analytics plus demand forecasting in one dashboard.
pros: Machine-learning forecasts up to three weeks out, more accurate than rolling averages
Unifies sales, labor, inventory, and reviews into one view
Pulls in weather, holidays, and local events
Quick setup and low entry price
cons: Needs at least ~90 days of history to forecast
Per-module, per-location pricing climbs
Analytics-led; not a scheduling or inventory system itself
integrations: Aggregates data from POS, labor planners, inventory tools, review platforms, and external weather/events feeds.
verdict: A strong one-pane-of-glass for operators who want forecasting and analytics together. The pricing grows with modules and sites, so scope what you actually need.

pricing: Quote-only; no transparent public tiers. Existing estimates put it roughly $150-$400/mo. Newer, smaller vendor; request a demo for current pricing.
bestFor: Operators who want ML demand forecasts translated into concrete labor and purchasing decisions.
pros: Forecasts sales up to ~21 days out with high stated accuracy
Turns forecasts into specific staffing and ordering calls
Integrates with POS and labor-scheduling systems
Positioned as affordable for what it does
cons: No public pricing
Newer and smaller than established rivals
Best results require clean integrated data
integrations: Integrates with POS and labor-scheduling/purchasing systems to act on its forecasts.
verdict: Appealing because it pushes past forecasts into actual staffing and ordering recommendations. As a younger vendor with quote-only pricing, get a demo and check the integrations you need.

pricing: Free Comp plan (up to 15 employees, one location). Paid per location/month: Essentials ~$39.99 (up to 30 employees), Pro ~$79.99 (unlimited), Premium ~$134.99 plus $6/employee for payroll. Add-ons like tip pooling (~$24.99) and task management (~$12.99) stack. Prices rose in late 2025.
bestFor: Restaurants of any size that want scheduling, labor forecasting, tips, and team chat in one restaurant-native app.
pros: Free tier covers a small single-location team
Restaurant-specific scheduling with labor forecasting
Team chat, availability, and time-off built in
Fast payback from less overtime and saved admin time
cons: Add-ons (tips, tasks, payroll) stack cost per location
Per-location pricing climbs for multi-unit
Prices increased in 2025
integrations: Integrates with major POS systems (Toast, Square, Clover and others) and payroll providers.
verdict: The most restaurant-focused scheduler, with a genuinely useful free tier to start. Watch the add-ons, since tips and tasks can quietly double the per-location bill.

pricing: Free plan for one location (basic scheduling and time tracking). Paid per location/month: Essentials ~$24.95, Plus ~$59.95, All-in-One ~$99.95 (all unlimited employees). Payroll add-on ~$39/mo base plus $6/active employee.
bestFor: Single-location small businesses that want free scheduling and time tracking plus optional payroll and hiring.
pros: Free for one location, covering the basics well
Per-location pricing with unlimited employees (no per-head fee on core plans)
Adds time tracking, hiring, and payroll as you grow
Easy for non-technical owners
cons: Per-location pricing jumps the moment you add a second site
Less restaurant-specific forecasting than 7shifts
Best HR/compliance features only on the top tier
integrations: Integrates with common POS systems and payroll providers; broad small-business tool support.
verdict: Hard to beat for a single location thanks to the free tier and unlimited employees. If you run multiple sites, the per-location cost adds up fast, so compare with Deputy or Sling.

pricing: Per user/month: Scheduling ~$4.50, Premium ~$6 (scheduling + time/attendance), Pro ~$9, plus Enterprise custom. Minimum ~$30/mo per invoice (from Sept 2025). Analytics+ add-on ~$1.50/user/mo. A weekly per-active-user option can lower cost for variable teams.
bestFor: Fast-casual and full-service operators who want demand-based auto-scheduling at a low per-user price.
pros: Low per-user entry pricing
Demand-based auto-scheduling and forecasting
Biometric/kiosk clock-ins and attendance
Flexible weekly billing for variable staffing
cons: Per-user cost rises with large hourly teams
Minimum monthly spend introduced in 2025
Some advanced reporting is a paid add-on
integrations: Integrates with major POS and payroll systems; broad app ecosystem.
verdict: Strong demand-based scheduling at a friendly per-user price for smaller teams. The math flips for large hourly rosters, where per-location tools may be cheaper.

pricing: Free plan for up to 30 users (scheduling and communication). Premium ~$1.70/user/mo and Business ~$4/user/mo ($3.40 with annual billing); 15% off annual on paid plans. You only pay for users on the platform.
bestFor: Single sites and small teams that want free or very cheap scheduling and labor-cost tracking.
pros: Generous free tier (up to 30 users)
Very low per-user paid pricing
Shift swaps, time-off, and labor-cost tools
Cheap to scale as the team grows
cons: Lighter forecasting and analytics than rivals
Free plan's mobile app is view-only
Less restaurant-specific than 7shifts
integrations: Integrates with common POS and payroll tools; part of the Toast family of products.
verdict: The cheapest credible way to run scheduling for a small team, with a strong free tier. Step up to 7shifts or Deputy if you need real demand forecasting.

pricing: Subscription from about $16/mo per screen (existing estimate $15-$39/screen depending on tier). Runs on standard tablets (iOS 13+/Android 7+), so you can avoid proprietary hardware costs.
bestFor: Independents and small shops that want an affordable, flexible kitchen display that works with their existing POS.
pros: Very low per-screen pricing
Runs on off-the-shelf tablets, cutting hardware cost
Works with Square, Clover and other POS systems
Quick payback from fewer mistakes and faster tickets
cons: Fewer enterprise/multi-unit controls
Lighter analytics than high-end KDS
You supply and maintain the tablets
integrations: Integrates with Square, Clover, and other POS platforms; hardware-agnostic on common tablets.
verdict: The best-value KDS for an independent: cheap, flexible, and POS-agnostic. If you need deep multi-unit prep timing and analytics, look at ConnectSmart instead.

pricing: Quote-only and tied to your Toast POS plan ($0-$69/mo, custom plans available; many restaurants run $165+/mo per terminal). Runs on Toast displays (~$799+) or iPad; KDS is bundled into higher Toast packages and priced via quote.
bestFor: Restaurants already running Toast POS that want a deeply integrated kitchen display.
pros: Seamless if you already use Toast POS
Routes POS, online, and handheld orders to kitchen screens
One vendor and one support line
Tight ticket flow across order channels
cons: Locked to the Toast ecosystem
Opaque, quote-only pricing
Proprietary hardware adds upfront and replacement cost
integrations: Native to Toast POS, online ordering, and handhelds; not designed to work outside Toast.
verdict: The obvious choice if you're committed to Toast, with the smoothest integration. If you're not on Toast, the lock-in and hardware cost make standalone options more sensible.

pricing: Quote-only; custom by restaurant size and features. Existing estimate is roughly $50-$150/mo per screen. Now part of Crunchtime; integrates with 80+ POS partners.
bestFor: Full-service and multi-unit operations that need sophisticated ticket routing, prep timing, and kitchen analytics.
pros: Enterprise-grade kitchen coordination and ticket routing
Prep timing and detailed kitchen analytics
Integrates with 80+ POS systems
Backed by Crunchtime's operations suite
cons: Cost and complexity are high for small shops
Quote-only pricing
Some users find the price hard to justify below scale
integrations: Integrates with 80+ POS partners (Revel, Lightspeed and others); part of the Crunchtime ecosystem.
verdict: The most sophisticated KDS here, ideal for complex or multi-unit kitchens that need precise coordination. For a single small restaurant, it's more system and cost than necessary.
